source.title: Multistate and functional protein design using RoseTTAFold sequence space diffusion

initial_request.research_problem: Developing a sequence-space diffusion model (ProteinGenerator) based on RoseTTAFold that simultaneously generates protein sequences and structures, enabling direct sequence-based guidance for multistate and functional protein design.
initial_request.known_context: Structure-based diffusion models (RFdiffusion, Chroma) provide limited opportunities for sequence-based guidance and multistate design.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Structure-based diffusion models (RFdiffusion, Chroma) provide limited opportunities for sequence-based guidance and multistate design.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: How to enable sequence-based guidance and multistate design in protein diffusion models.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Carrying out diffusion in sequence space rather than structure space could enable guidance using sequence-based features and explicit design of sequences populating multiple states.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Develop a sequence-space diffusion model (ProteinGenerator) based on RoseTTAFold.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Sequence-space diffusion could overcome limitations of structure-based models.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Proceed to design sequence representation and diffusion process.

evidence.quote_or_span: Although powerful, structure-based approaches, such as RFdiffusion $^{11}$ and Chroma $^{12}$ , provide limited opportunities to guide protein generation using sequence-based features and to identify sequences with more than one fold and/or function.

We reasoned that carrying out diffusion in sequence space rather than structure space could enable guidance of design using sequence-based features and explicit design of sequences populating multiple states.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Sequence representation and noise schedule defined.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Need to train the model to generate sequences and structures.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Multi-task learning with diffusion, structure prediction, and fixed backbone design will yield a model capable of sequence-structure generation.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Train RoseTTAFold-based model with multi-task learning (40% diffusion, 30% structure prediction, 30% fixed backbone design) using categorical cross-entropy, FAPE, and KL losses with self-conditioning.

parameters.task_weights: diffusion 40%, structure prediction 30%, fixed backbone design 30%

evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Set up inference procedure with self-conditioning and logit clamping.

evidence.quote_or_span: Along with the standard diffusion task (40% of the time), the model was also challenged with structure prediction (seq2str) and fixed backbone sequence design (30% of the time each).

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Does unconditional generation produce viable proteins?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Unconditional generation will yield a fraction of high-confidence designs that are soluble and monomeric.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Generate proteins unconditionally and filter by AF2 pLDDT>90 and RMSD<2Å; experimentally test 42 designs.
evolution_trajectory.observation: 6% of designs have AF2 pLDDT>90 and RMSD<2Å; 32/42 tested proteins soluble and monomeric.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Explore sequence-based guidance with rare amino acid enrichment.

evidence.quote_or_span: 6% of designs have AF2 confidence pLDDT > 90 and RMSD < 2 Å
Of the 42 proteins experimentally tested, 32 were soluble and monomeric by SEC
Designs were filtered by AF2 pLDDT > 90 and AF2 RMSD to design < 2 Å for ordering final constructs.

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Can sequence-based guidance enrich rare amino acids and induce disulfide bonds without structural conditioning?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Biasing amino acid composition during generation will yield soluble proteins enriched in rare amino acids, and high cysteine content will form disulfide bonds.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Generate proteins with biased amino acid composition (tryptophan, cysteine, valine, histidine, methionine) and test expression and solubility.
evolution_trajectory.observation: 68/96 expressed designs soluble; cysteine designs formed 3-4 disulfide bonds without structural conditioning.

evidence.quote_or_span: Of the expressed designs, 68 were soluble in Escherichia coli
Guiding for high cysteine content at the sequence level resulted in the formation of 3–4 disulfide bonds per protein without any structural conditioning
separate PG trajectories were carried out for each enriched amino acid.
SEC Normalized absorbance
Generated designs were filtered for high AF2 confidence (pLDDT > 90) and self-consistency (RMSD to design < 2 Å), and 96 were selected for experimental characterization.
For broad applicability, we carried out this optimization with standard settings and no extensive hyperparameter turning (96 designs generated and tested per round for three rounds).

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Can PG generate diverse repeat proteins?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Secondary structure conditioning will enable diverse repeat protein designs beyond beta-solenoids.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Generate repeat proteins with and without secondary structure conditioning; test solubility and monomericity.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Unconditional generation biased toward beta-solenoids; secondary structure conditioning enabled diverse designs; 27/74 capped and 10/86 uncapped soluble and monomeric.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Validate atomic accuracy with crystal structure.

evidence.quote_or_span: Unconditional generation with this approach yielded largely beta-solenoid structures.

To encourage further exploration, we trained PG to condition on secondary structure (Supplementary Fig. 16) and specified secondary structure constraints to yield a wide range of all-alpha, all-beta and mixed alpha/beta designs
Of these, 27 repeats with caps and 10 repeats without helical caps were soluble and monomeric by SEC

evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: The designed repeat protein structure will match the crystal structure with high accuracy.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Solve crystal structure of a five-repeat four-helix bundle design.
evolution_trajectory.observation: C RMSD 1.38 Å overall, 0.47 Å for asymmetric unit.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Apply PG to cage bioactive peptides.

evidence.quote_or_span: We solved the crystal structure of a five-repeat unit design composed of a four-helix bundle asymmetric unit and found the design to have atomic accuracy: the C RMSD of design to the crystal structure was 1.38 Å for the whole structure and 0.47 Å for the asymmetric unit
Diffraction quality crystals formed in a mixture of 0.1 M PCB buffer (pH 4) and 25% PEG 1500.

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Can PG cage bioactive peptides for conditional activity?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Fixing a bioactive peptide sequence and diffusing the remainder with secondary structure conditioning and a furin cleavage site will yield a caged peptide that is released upon protease cleavage.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Design melittin cages by fixing melittin sequence, diffusing scaffold, and including furin cleavage site.

evidence.quote_or_span: We used PG to address this challenge by scaffolding bioactive peptide sequences within an inert protein cage (Fig. 4a) by designating a region of the protein chain (usually at the N or C terminus) to be fixed at the bioactive peptide sequence and freely diffusing the remainder of the sequence.
Proteins of 155 amino acids in length were generated in 25 steps with spans of helical DSSP conditioning to encourage the model to generate helical bundles and cleavage loops.

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Do the designed cages express, fold, and release melittin upon furin cleavage?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: A subset of designs will be soluble, monomeric, helical, thermostable, and show conditional hemolysis.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Express and characterize 13 designs; test furin cleavage and hemolysis.
evolution_trajectory.observation: 5/13 soluble, monomeric, helical, thermostable; furin cleavage confirmed; conditional hemolysis (P=0.002, P=0.005).
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Note limitation of increased sampling and filtering.

evidence.quote_or_span: Of 13 experimentally characterized designs, five were soluble and monodisperse by SEC, folded with helical secondary structure by CD and thermostable
Upon addition of furin protease, a band shift from -18 kD to -15 kD indicated cleavage of our designed melittin cage
Mass spectrometry analysis confirmed release of intact melittin peptide
Of 13 experimentally characterized designs, five were soluble and monodisperse by SEC, folded with helical secondary structure by CD and thermostable

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Can PG scaffold barcode peptides for protein identification?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: PG can scaffold short barcode sequences into a protein, enabling intrinsic barcoding.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Design proteins with C-terminal barcode sequences and test expression and monodispersity.

evidence.quote_or_span: PG could scaffold short barcode-like sequences (7–14 residues) into a protein of interest, thereby removing the need for multiple extrinsic barcodes
64 of 84 (76%) were expressed, and 48 of 64 (75%) of the expressed proteins exhibited monodisperse elution peaks
We observed close agreement between SEC-MS and iSEC peak elution volume for 41 of 58 (71%) designs
Proteins of 100 amino acids in length were generated in 50 steps with barcodes being set as fixed regions on the C-terminus.

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Do the designs adopt the intended parent and child folds?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: A subset of parent-child triples will be soluble and monodisperse, and NMR structures will match designs.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Characterize 72 triples; select 4 families for CD and NMR; solve NMR structures of MS2 and MS3 parents.
evolution_trajectory.observation: MS2 parent Cα RMSD 1.06 Å, MS3 parent Cα RMSD 1.61 Å.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Apply PG to iterative optimization with experimental data.

evidence.quote_or_span: We experimentally characterized 72 parent–child triples that AF2 predicted with high confidence and accuracy to be in the parent state when intact and the child states when split
we selected 4 (MS1–MS4) soluble and monodisperse sequence families for detailed CD and nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) studies
The solution NMR structure of MS2 parent is within 1.06 Å Cα RMSD of the design model
The solution NMR structure of MS3 parent is within 1.61 Å Cα RMSD of the design model

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Can PG iteratively optimize sequences using experimental fitness data?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Gradient-based guidance from classifiers trained on experimental fitness will outperform Bayesian optimization.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Simulate 3 rounds of iterative design on GB1 fitness landscape; compare to Bayesian optimization.
evolution_trajectory.observation: PG outperforms Bayesian optimization in average and maximum fitness (P<0.05).
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Attribute improved performance to RoseTTAFold prior.

evidence.quote_or_span: In silico demonstration of iterative design using GB1 fitness landscape for binding and comparison with Bayesian optimization (BO).
Mean activities for each round are statistically significant between the two populations (P < 0.05, two-sided Mann–Whitney U-test, n = 96 designs per round).
We simulated an iterative guidance process by exploring the fitness landscape of the protein GB1 via gradient-based optimization with IgG binding activity-guided diffusion trajectories.

We found that the average and maximum fitness of PG-generated designs increases each round, outperforming a Bayesian optimization baseline with the best identified acquisition function (batched upper confidence bound)

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Why does PG outperform Bayesian optimization?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: The rich sequence-structure prior in RoseTTAFold enables better exploration.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Attribute improved performance to RoseTTAFold prior.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Improved performance likely due to RoseTTAFold's prior understanding of sequence-structure relationships.

evidence.quote_or_span: The improved performance likely reflects the rich prior understanding of protein sequence–structure relationships implicit in RoseTTAFold compared to the baseline, which has access to only limited experimental data.

success_verification.final_conclusion: ProteinGenerator (PG) successfully generates sequences and structures with sequence-based guidance, enabling multistate design, peptide caging, barcode scaffolding, and iterative optimization, outperforming Bayesian optimization on GB1.
limitations: Considerable filtering required for complex designs (e.g., melittin cages).
PG better for soft sequence constraints; structure-based models better for global structural properties.
